name: The movie houses of the Avenue
certaintyNote: Rizal Avenue, the Avenida, is named and real; its grand cinemas clustered in the Santa Cruz stretch. The exact theatre is not fixed here, so this marks a representative point along the avenue's old movie-palace row.
note: The novel opens with the young narrator and her cousin at the pictures, an American melodrama flickering in a cooled, gilded hall. Those palaces stood along Rizal Avenue, the downtown thoroughfare Manilenos called the Avenida, whose picture houses defined the city's imported dream life in the 1950s.

sources.ref: Rizal Avenue, Wikipedia, on the Avenida and its Santa Cruz cinema district
List of Art Deco theaters in Metro Manila, Wikipedia, on the movie palaces of the Avenue

name: Escolta
certaintyNote: Escolta is a named, real street in Binondo; coordinates mark the historic commercial stretch by the Pasig.
note: The narrator's Manila is also a city of shopping and display, the smart downtown where old money and new appetite meet. Escolta, the colonial commercial street in Binondo just north of the river, was the city's premier address for department stores and cafes, the setting for that world of imported goods and status.

sources.ref: Escolta Street, Wikidata, for the street's location in Binondo
The Cinemas and Theaters of Old Manila, TriptheIslands, on downtown Manila's commercial heart

name: The nightclubs of Ermita
certaintyNote: The novel's bars and clubs are not tied to one named address; Ermita and its Mabini Street strip were Manila's nightlife quarter in the Marcos era, so this marks that district as a representative point.
note: Night in the novel belongs to hostesses, hustlers and foreign visitors moving through smoky clubs and hotel bars. Ermita, along Mabini Street south of the old walled city, was Manila's nightlife district in these years, the natural ground for those neon-lit scenes.

sources.ref: Ermita, Wikipedia, on the district's coordinates and its nightlife strip
Ermita and Malate Nightlife, The Trip Guru, on the Mabini Street nightlife area

name: Tondo
certaintyNote: Tondo is a named, real district of Manila; coordinates mark the district centre in the city's northwest.
note: Against the palaces and clubs the novel sets the other Manila, the crowded poverty from which some of its characters climb and to which the city keeps returning. Tondo, in the northwest by the port, was the largest and most densely packed of the city's districts, shorthand for the slums the dictatorship's glamour tried to hide.

sources.ref: Tondo, Manila, latlong.info, for the district coordinates

name: Malacanang Palace
certaintyNote: Malacanang, the presidential palace, is a named, real building on the Pasig in San Miguel; coordinates mark the palace itself.
note: At the top of the novel's world sits the presidential palace, source of the favours, the fear and the violence that reach every other place on this map. Malacanang stands on the north bank of the Pasig in San Miguel, the seat of the Marcos-era power the book anatomises.

sources.ref: Malacanang Palace, Wikidata, for the palace coordinates
GPS coordinates for Malacanang Palace, CoordinatesFinder.com
